comments:
Summary
This paper introduces the concept of "elastic functions" to lift functions on time series (under dynamic time warping, DTW) to functions on a matrix (Euclidean) space, which are piecewise smooth. By doing so the author(s) apply generalized gradient methods (Ermoliev–Norkin framework) to learn parameters for models defined on time series, including elastic variants of logistic regression, perceptron (with margin), and linear SVM. Convergence results (consistency and an elastic perceptron convergence theorem) are stated. The approach is generalized to multivariate sequences, symbolic sequences via embedding, and other elastic proximities; experiments evaluate the four elastic linear classifiers on two-class problems from the UCR benchmark and compare to nearest-neighbor DTW baselines with various prototype sets.

Strengths
- The paper addresses an important gap: gradient-based learning under elastic alignments like DTW. The conceptual device of elastic functions is a clear and potentially useful way to connect DTW-style alignment to piecewise-smooth optimization.
- The work ties existing non-smooth optimization theory (generalized gradients) to learning on time series and provides convergence statements, including an elastic perceptron convergence result.
- The treatment covers useful generalizations (multivariate, symbolic sequences, other local costs) and provides a unifying view that explains and connects prior mean/prototype-based schemes.
- Experiments on the UCR two-class problems are reasonably extensive and show that simple elastic linear classifiers can be competitive with NN+DTW baselines while being computationally cheaper than NN with many prototypes.

Weaknesses and Issues
- Presentation quality: the provided text contains repeated paragraphs and some formatting irregularities (likely PDF->text artifacts). Definitions and some arguments are concise but could be clearer; additional illustrative examples or figures would help.
- The experimental comparisons are somewhat limited. The baselines are NN+DTW variants (ALL, KME, AHC), which are sensible, but important comparisons are missing: (a) stronger/modern time-series classifiers or ensembles (e.g., elastic ensembles acknowledged in text), (b) other learned model baselines such as nearest-neighbor with learned prototypes tailored to classification, or kernel/SVM methods that use DTW-aware kernels. There is limited reporting of statistical significance or variance across runs, though the author repeated trials and used cross-validation for hyperparameters.
- The choice of the matrix width parameter m (set to 0.1·n) appears ad-hoc. The paper would benefit from ablation studies showing sensitivity to m, to regularization, and to constraints like Sakoe–Chiba band width. Also runtime/complexity measurements (wall-clock or asymptotic constants) are not reported in detail; the claim that EL classifiers are faster than NN in practice depends on number of prototypes and on lower-bounding/acceleration usage.
- Some theoretical arguments are presented at a high level (relying on existing generalized-gradient results). The elastic perceptron convergence theorem is plausible, but the proof sketch depends on a bounded-subgradient constant C; it would strengthen the work to state clearly the technical assumptions needed (e.g., bounds on embedded norms, conditions guaranteeing finiteness of C) and to supply more detailed proofs or appendices.
- The experiments focus only on two-class UCR datasets; it is unclear how methods scale to multiclass problems, very long sequences, or to noisy/real-world datasets outside UCR.

Suggestions for Improvement
1. Clean up presentation: remove duplicated text, add clear notation summary, and include illustrative figures showing an embedding example, the matrix parameter W, and active warping paths.
2. Expand experimental evaluation: add comparisons to additional baselines (e.g., elastic ensembles, DTW-based SVM kernels, learned-shapelet or representation learning methods), include multiclass datasets, report runtime (training and inference) and memory use, and include sensitivity/ablation studies for m, regularization, and warping constraints.
3. Provide a more detailed discussion of computational complexity of the proposed updates (per-iteration costs) and of practical acceleration strategies.
4. Strengthen theoretical exposition: explicitly state assumptions required to bound subgradients and ensure conditions of Ermoliev–Norkin apply; include full proofs or detailed appendices for the main theorems.
5. Discuss limitations more explicitly: cases where elastic-linear decision boundaries are insufficient and how to extend to richer nonlinear architectures (the paper gestures at single-layer nets but does not evaluate them).

Overall assessment
The paper presents an interesting and fairly original conceptual contribution (elastic functions) that meaningfully connects DTW-style alignments to non-smooth optimization and enables gradient-like learning on time series. The theoretical framing is sound in broad strokes, and the experiments suggest promise for simple elastic linear classifiers. The manuscript would be stronger with clearer presentation, broader empirical comparisons, and fuller technical detail in proofs and assumptions. I recommend acceptance to a venue where there is space for an expanded version (or acceptance as a short paper with encouragement to address the empirical and exposition gaps).
